chunks.text: Edwin R. Lewis is Professor Emeritus at UC Berkeley EECS. His contact information includes 565 Cory Hall, email lewis@eecs.berkeley.edu, and office hours by email appointment only. He has a personal homepage, publications list, and dissertation information available.

Edwin Lewis earned AB (Biology), MS (EE), and Engineer (EE) degrees from Stanford in 1956-1959. His PhD research was in semiconductor physical electronics at Stanford's Applied Electronics Lab. In 1961, he worked with RF Reiss and TH Bullock to reverse engineer nervous systems. He joined UC Berkeley's EE faculty in July 1967, retired from teaching in 2000, but continued research. His primary research at Cal was reverse engineering nervous systems, focusing on the vertebrate inner ear.

In 1968, Lewis established an ecology course for engineers and taught it for about a decade until ecology became part of Civil Engineering. He was immersed in campus ecology, participating in teaching, advising, research, and guiding graduate students. He also advised the Grasslands Biome part of the US/IBP. Lewis oversaw the bioelectronics option for EE undergraduates and taught related courses until retirement. He participated in teaching cellular neurobiology and Introduction to Bioengineering courses, co-editing a text in 1996.

From 1969 to 1977, Lewis was PI of the NIH Biomedical Engineering Training Grant. In 1982-83, he co-founded the Joint UC Berkeley-UCSF Graduate Program in Bioengineering. From 1988 to 1996, he served as Associate Dean for Interdisciplinary Studies and chaired the program's Executive Committee. In 1983, he revised the undergraduate Bioengineering Science program, making it highly competitive. This inspired the Engineering Undeclared option implemented in 1996.

Lewis chaired the Faculty Senate Committee on Research (1973-76), was Associate Dean for Research (1977-82), and served on advisory boards including Lawrence Berkeley National Lab (1978-85) and USC's Biomedical Simulations Resource (1995-2006). Research descriptions are on his personal homepage. He mentored 26 PhD students and many undergraduates/MS students. He is a Fellow of IEEE, Acoustical Society of America, and Biomedical Engineering Society Emeritus.

Lewis received an NIH Jacob Javits Neuroscience Investigatorship in 1984, a UC Berkeley Distinguished Teaching Award in 1972, and the Berkeley Citation in 1997. His education includes a Ph.D. in Electrical Engineering from Stanford in 1959, an M.S. in EE from Stanford in 1957, and an A.B. in Biology from Stanford in 1956. His research areas are Biosystems & Computational Biology and sensory neurobiology.
